Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty chaotic because I was juggling so much with limited resources. I usually tried to block out time on weekends and a couple of evenings during the week, aiming for about 15 hours total. I'm a visual learner, so I'd spend a lot of time creating diagrams and color-coded notes from the materials. Having nine online courses meant I had to be strategic about which ones got my attention each week, which was exhausting but necessary.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on deadlines and what else was going on in my life. Some weeks I'd be really consistent, studying at the same times, but then a big assignment would be due and everything would shift to crisis mode. Financial pressures meant I sometimes had to prioritize work over studying at regular times. I tried to maintain some consistency, but honestly, it was more about survival than routine.
Q3: Usually what prompted me to log in was checking if there were new assignments posted or if grades had been released. I'd also log in when I needed to watch a specific lecture video or find materials for an assignment I was working on. Sometimes my peer study group would message me about something on the LMS and that would remind me to check in. It wasn't really a habit I enjoyed—more like a necessary task.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never became something I naturally gravitated toward as part of my routine. I used it when I absolutely had to, but it felt more like a tool I accessed out of obligation than something integrated into my daily life. The peer support group I connected with helped remind me to check things, which was probably the closest it got to feeling routine. Without those nudges from classmates, I probably would have logged in even less.
Q5: The lecture videos were definitely the most useful for me since I learn best visually. I'd watch them and take detailed visual notes, pausing frequently to draw out concepts. The assignment submission portal was obviously essential, and I appreciated being able to see my grades there. Discussion forums were occasionally helpful when peers explained things in different ways than the instructor. Anything with diagrams or visual explanations really clicked for me.
Q6: I often ignored the discussion forums beyond what was required because they felt time-consuming and I was already stretched thin. Some of the supplementary readings I'd skip if they weren't directly tied to assignments—I just didn't have the bandwidth. The optional quizzes I rarely touched because my anxiety was already high enough without adding more self-assessment opportunities. I had to be ruthless about what got my limited time and energy.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to improving my assignment grades or understanding core concepts I knew would be on exams. I needed to see a clear payoff for the time invested because I couldn't afford to waste hours on things that wouldn't help my grades. Visual resources that helped me understand complex ideas quickly were always worth it. If something felt like busywork or wasn't clearly tied to my success, I'd skip it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford, honestly. Required activities got my full attention because they directly impacted my grades, but optional ones always got pushed to the bottom of my priority list. There was this constant calculation in my head about whether the time spent would actually benefit me enough. With my anxiety levels being what they are, adding optional tasks just felt like more opportunities to stress myself out.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my actual studying happened offline. I'd download lecture slides and videos when I could, then study from those without being connected. I created extensive handwritten notes with diagrams and visual maps that I'd review constantly. I also used free resources from the library and borrowed textbooks from peers since I couldn't always afford to buy my own. A lot of my learning happened through study sessions with my peer support group that obviously wouldn't show up anywhere online.
Q10: There were many times I'd be deep in studying but the LMS would show nothing. I'd spend hours reviewing my handwritten notes and visual diagrams I'd created from downloaded materials. My peer study group would meet in person at the library, and we'd work through problems together for hours. I'd also watch downloaded videos multiple times while commuting or during breaks at work. The LMS only captured the initial download, not the repeated engagement with those materials.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my standard approach. I'd access a video or document once, download it, and then use it repeatedly offline over days or weeks. My internet access isn't always reliable, so downloading things when I could was essential. I'd return to the same lecture videos multiple times while preparing for exams, but the LMS would only show that single initial access. It was a survival strategy that worked well for my learning but made my engagement look minimal.
Q12: There were definitely periods when my anxiety spiked and engaging with anything felt overwhelming. Around midterms, I remember feeling completely paralyzed by the workload across all nine courses. Financial stress would sometimes hit hard and I'd have to pick up extra shifts, which meant the course got pushed aside. My attendance dropped during those times because I physically couldn't be everywhere I needed to be. The peer support intervention actually helped pull me through some of those darker moments.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from working or stressed about money, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to look at. Cognitive overload from managing nine courses meant I had to triage constantly, and sometimes this course got less attention. Confusion about certain concepts would make me avoid the materials entirely because facing them felt too anxiety-inducing. I'd sometimes just download what I needed quickly and log off because spending more time on the platform felt draining.
Q14: Oh, for sure. Sometimes I'd be scrolling through materials but my brain was completely elsewhere, worrying about bills or other assignments. There were times I'd have a video playing but I wasn't actually absorbing anything—I'd have to rewatch it later when I could focus. During high-anxiety periods, I'd go through the motions of accessing content without really engaging with it. It was like my body was present but my mind was too overwhelmed to actually learn.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts constantly because they triggered my anxiety about being judged by peers. Even though I knew participating was important, the thought of putting my ideas out there felt terrifying some days. I'd also avoid starting big assignments until closer to deadlines because the scope felt overwhelming. It wasn't laziness—it was more like self-protection from feeling inadequate. The peer support group helped me push through some of that avoidance.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my entire LMS usage pattern. I'd have bursts of intense activity in the days before something was due, then go quiet afterward. The deadline pressure actually helped me focus despite my anxiety because there was no choice but to engage. I'd log in more frequently as due dates approached, checking requirements and submitting work. Without deadlines, I honestly might not have engaged with the LMS much at all.
Q17: Feedback was huge for my motivation. When I got good grades back, it gave me this boost of confidence that helped push through the anxiety. Seeing that I scored well on assignments made me feel like my strategies were working despite not being super active on the platform. Negative or unclear feedback would sometimes send me into a spiral though, making me avoid that part of the course for a while. The peer support helped me process feedback more constructively.
Q18: The course structure with clear deadlines and organized modules helped me plan my limited study time effectively. Having materials available for download was essential given my situation. However, having nine courses simultaneously meant I couldn't engage as deeply as the structure might have intended. The flexibility to work at my own pace was crucial because my schedule was so unpredictable. I appreciated that attendance wasn't the only factor in my grade.
Q19: The visual resources and well-organized modules made engagement easier for someone who learns the way I do. Having peer interaction opportunities built into the course helped because my support group formed through those connections. What made it harder was when content was only available in text-heavy formats without visual aids. The volume of content across all my courses sometimes made it hard to know where to focus my limited energy.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious or overwhelmed and keeps checking things without actually being able to commit to submitting work. They might be searching for clarity or reassurance, logging in repeatedly but feeling too paralyzed to actually complete tasks. It could also be someone who's struggling with the material and keeps reviewing without feeling confident enough to submit. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity—sometimes it's just anxiety manifesting.
Q21: That absolutely reflects my experience. I performed well despite relatively low LMS activity because my real learning happened offline with downloaded materials and peer study sessions. The platform captured maybe 20% of my actual engagement with the course content. Someone looking at my login frequency would think I wasn't trying, but I was working really hard—just not in ways the system could see. Quality of engagement matters more than quantity of clicks.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when someone accessed materials and submitted work, which gives a basic timeline of engagement. They capture patterns around deadlines pretty well. But they completely miss offline studying, the quality of attention during access, emotional state while engaging, and learning that happens through peer collaboration outside the platform. They also can't distinguish between someone mindlessly scrolling and someone deeply focused during the same login session.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions to get a grade. It's when I'm making connections between concepts, asking questions, and wanting to learn more. For me, it involves creating those visual notes and diagrams that help cement my understanding. It's also about being part of a learning community, which my peer support group provided. True engagement is mental and emotional investment, not just time spent logged in.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I grew in resilience throughout this course despite my anxiety and limited resources. It wouldn't capture the hours spent in peer study sessions that were crucial to my success, or the emotional support that kept me going. My offline study habits—all those handwritten visual notes and repeated video reviews—are invisible to the system. It also can't show how hard I had to fight through anxiety to submit each assignment, or how much the peer support intervention genuinely helped my wellbeing. My distinction grade came from determination the data simply can't measure.
